original_abstract: While the mission of Jesus in Judea (Jn 3:22–36) is often overlooked in contemporary scholarship, its geographical placement is central to understanding the Gospel’s narrative trajectory. This study examines the spatial structure of John 2:13–4:54, suggesting it follows a pattern analogous to the missional movement found in Acts 1:8. In the Johannine account, Jesus’ ministry shifts from the religious center of Jerusalem toward the Judean countryside, subsequently reaching Samaria and Galilee. This movement from the center to the periphery ensures that all segments of Israel are presented with the message of faith prior to the escalating conflicts with authorities detailed in later chapters. Within this geographical framework, the identity of the anonymous "Jew" (Ἰουδαῖος) in John 3:25—who is engaged in a dispute with the disciples of John the Baptist—takes on new significance. By situating the term within the specific "land of Judea," this paper argues that Ἰουδαῖος should be interpreted primarily as a "Judean," reflecting a regional rather than a strictly religious designation. Such a reading provides clarity to the verse and integrates the episode more firmly into Jesus’ systematic mission to the whole of Israel.
humanized_abstract: The mission of Jesus in Judea (Jn 3:22–36) is not often a focus in modern Jesus studies, yet it is profoundly significant for the geography of the Gospel of John. This paper examines the spatial dynamics of John 2:13–4:54 and explores the possibility that this section organises the mission of Jesus within a spatial dynamics that echoes the missional structure of Acts 1:8. Specifically, the geography of the Gospel of John organises the mission of Jesus by moving progressively from the centre (Jerusalem) to the periphery (Judea, Samaria and Galilee), so that all parts of Israel hear the gospel before the tension with the Jewish authorities that characterises the second part of the Gospel is introduced. Furthermore, the use of the term 'Jew' in John 3:25, in relation to the dispute between the Judean disciples and those of John the Baptist, also becomes significant when one realises that 'land of Judea' functions as the spatial referent in this context means that the term Ἰουδαῖος in John 3:25 is not necessarily an ethnic term but a regional term for a Judean, a term that in this context organises the episode into the mission of Jesus to all of Israel in a way that illuminates the scripture.
